metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Evidence assertion about `list_tickets` error propagation: `list_by_query` does NOT wrap `ticket_reads.list_states` exceptions as `RebarError`; the retry must use a broad `except Exception` predicate (as the plan's Remediation item 1 already specifies) rather than catching only `RebarError`.
evidence: src/rebar/_reads.py:65–76: `list_by_query` calls `ticket_reads.list_states(tracker, query)` with NO try/except — exceptions from list_states propagate unwrapped.
src/rebar/_reads.py:53–62: `show_ticket` DOES wrap `ticket_reads.ReadError` → `RebarError`, but `list_by_query`/`list_tickets` does not mirror this pattern.

The plan uses this claim to justify 'a bespoke tiny retry is justified' — the broad `except Exception` in `_assemble_context_uncached` already handles whatever propagates, so the remediation is still correct, but the premise about the error type is wrong.

Consequence: the retry predicate for `list_tickets` cannot rely on catching `RebarError`; it must catch `Exception` broadly as the existing code already does — which the plan's own retry spec says to do, but the stated reason ('single broad `RebarError`') is inaccurate.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `_reads.list_tickets` (and `list_by_query`) raises `RebarError` on any failure. In fact, `list_by_query` (the sole implementation called by `list_tickets`) invokes `ticket_reads.list_states(tracker, query)` with NO try/except wrapper, meaning `ReadError` or other exceptions from the read layer propagate as-is — not as `RebarError`. The claim 'raise a single broad `RebarError` on any failure' is therefore inaccurate for the `list_tickets` path.
findings.location: Evidence section, bullet: '`_reads.show_ticket`/`list_tickets` (`src/rebar/_reads.py:53-94`) raise a single broad `RebarError` on any failure'
scenarios: A reader implementing the retry for `list_tickets` might narrow the catch to `RebarError` based on this claim, missing `ticket_reads.ReadError` or other exceptions that actually propagate from `list_by_query`.
Test authors verifying the retry behavior might inject a `RebarError` when the actual failure mode is a `ReadError` or `IOError`.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Evidence bullet to: '`_reads.show_ticket` raises `RebarError` (wraps `ticket_reads.ReadError`); `_reads.list_tickets` delegates to `list_by_query` which calls `ticket_reads.list_states` with NO exception wrapper — propagating `ReadError` or other exceptions directly. Both paths are caught by the existing broad `except Exception` in `_assemble_context_uncached`; the retry predicate must remain equally broad.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `gates/plan-review.yaml` as an explicit edit target: all four `coach` step `with:` blocks must include `hierarchy_incomplete: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.hierarchy_incomplete }}` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.hierarchy_incomplete_detail }}` so `ctx.inputs.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` in `plan_review_coach` receives the precheck value rather than always returning the default `False`.
evidence: plan_review_coach reads: `ctx.inputs.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` and `ctx.inputs.get('hierarchy_incomplete_detail', [])` per Remediation item 2
ctx.inputs is populated from the YAML `with:` block — confirmed by reading workflow_ops.py:540-550 where coverage is built entirely from ctx.inputs.get(...)
plan-review.yaml coach step `with:` blocks confirmed at lines ~202-216 and ~298-310: they include det_coverage, routing, canonical_id, ticket_type — but no hierarchy_incomplete
The plan names only orchestrator.py, det_floor.py, workflow_ops.py, gate_dispatch.py, drift_floor.py, xcheck.py, and the precheck schema as edit targets — plan-review.yaml is never mentioned
findings.finding: `gates/plan-review.yaml` is an unenumerated edit target: the plan requires `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:503) to read `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` via `ctx.inputs.get(...)`, but `ctx.inputs` is populated solely from the YAML `with:` block. The plan-review YAML has four `coach` step instances (two branch arms × two review sub-branches, at lines ~202–216 and ~298–310), each with explicit `with:` inputs sourced from `steps.precheck.outputs`. None include `hierarchy_incomplete` or `hierarchy_incomplete_detail`. Without adding those two fields to all four `coach` `with:` blocks, `ctx.inputs.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` will always return `False`, silently defeating the entire coach-path threading.
scenarios: A container ticket whose child enumeration fails: precheck sets hierarchy_incomplete=True in its output dict; the YAML coach step never passes it to plan_review_coach; ctx.inputs.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False) returns False; the INDETERMINATE verdict is not set; a clean PASS is emitted — exactly the bug this ticket is supposed to fix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Remediation item 1 rationale: Python raises `TypeError` (not `ValueError`) for mutable `@dataclass` field defaults; update the parenthetical so the stated justification matches Python's actual behavior.
evidence: Plan quote: 'a bare = [] default on a @dataclass list field raises ValueError at class-definition time (matching the existing children: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list) pattern at det_floor.py:114)'
Python's dataclasses module raises TypeError (message: 'mutable default <class list> for field X is not allowed; use default_factory'), not ValueError
The prescription itself (field(default_factory=list)) is correct and the existing det_floor.py:114 already uses it — so the error is in the explanatory rationale, not the implementation instruction
findings.finding: The plan's Evidence/Remediation text asserts that a bare `= []` default on a `@dataclass` list field raises `ValueError` at class-definition time. Python actually raises `TypeError` (not `ValueError`) for mutable defaults in dataclasses. The prescription (use `field(default_factory=list)`) is correct and consistent with `det_floor.py:114`, but the stated rationale is a false capability assertion about Python's runtime behavior that could mislead implementers checking the justification.
findings.location: Remediation item 1 — '…a bare `= []` default on a `@dataclass` list field raises `ValueError` at class-definition time…'
scenarios: An implementer reads the plan's rationale, encounters 'ValueError' in a code search or test expectation, and writes a test that asserts the wrong exception type.
A reviewer auditing the plan's justification against Python docs flags the mislabeled exception and questions the plan's overall technical accuracy.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '…raises `ValueError` at class-definition time…' to '…raises `TypeError` at class-definition time (Python's dataclasses module: "mutable default <class list> for field X is not allowed; use default_factory")…'.

evidence: 6 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC group: (a) a `pytest` path (e.g., `pytest tests/plan_review/test_context_assembly.py -v`) for the retry/flag and verdict tests; (b) a `jq -e '.properties | has("hierarchy_incomplete") and has("hierarchy_incomplete_detail")' src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json` command for the schema AC; (c) an end-to-end invocation or integration test reference that exercises at least one of the five finalize_verdict sites producing INDETERMINATE and surviving a drift-floor drop.
evidence: Remediation item 7 describes test-case assertions ('add a total-enumeration-failure test asserting hierarchy_incomplete_detail == ["enumeration"] exactly') but gives no file path, no test module location, and no pytest invocation command.
No AC bullet contains a shell command, pytest marker, or grep/jq expression to produce verifiable evidence on success.
The schema AC ('plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json declares hierarchy_incomplete... additionalProperties: false is preserved') has no `jq` or `python -c json.load` command to verify it.
findings.finding: No concrete proving commands (e.g., `pytest` invocations with specific test-module paths, `grep`/`jq` commands against the schema file) are provided for any acceptance criterion. An executing agent completing this work cannot produce evidence of success without inferring which test files exist, what commands to run, and how to assert schema properties — none of which are specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all 10 bullet items); Remediation item 7 (test-case content assertions)
scenarios: An agent completes all code changes and signs off; a reviewer has no concrete artifact to run to confirm the INDETERMINATE verdict is produced end-to-end through all five finalize_verdict sites.
The schema change could introduce a typo in a field name with no failing command to catch it at completion time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure ALL return paths of `plan_review_precheck` include `hierarchy_incomplete` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` in the emitted dict — specifically the bug-tier return (lines 171-178) and the `run_llm=True` return (line 218) in addition to the P8-too-big/finalize_verdict path; the initial `base` dict (lines 141-147) should include both fields defaulting to `False`/`[]` so every `{**base, ...}` spread inherits them.
evidence: Bug-tier return (lines 171-178): `return {**base, 'det_blocking': [], ..., 'run_llm': True, 'verdict': None}` — base at this point is the initial dict from lines 141-147, which the plan does not mention updating with hierarchy fields
Recovery functions read `precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` where `precheck = succeeded.get(STEP_PRECHECK)` is the RECORDED step output dict

The plan's Remediation item 2 for `plan_review_precheck` says: 'reads pctx.hierarchy_incomplete/pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail directly... and threads both into its base/coverage dicts, used at its own finalize_verdict call (line 209)' — this only covers the P8-too-big arm, not the bug-tier arm

AC item: 'plan_review_precheck (workflow_ops.py:135) reads pctx.hierarchy_incomplete... and threads both into its base/coverage dicts' — the scope of 'base' is ambiguous across the multiple return paths
findings.finding: The `plan_review_precheck` bug-tier return path (workflow_ops.py lines 171-178) does not include `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` in its output dict, but the recovery functions `_recover_plan_review_coach_failure` and `_recover_plan_review_verify_failure` read these fields via `precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False)` from the RECORDED step output. A bug-tier ticket whose child enumeration fails will produce a precheck output dict without those keys; if the LLM pass subsequently fails and recovery runs, the hierarchy flag is lost and the recovered verdict omits the INDETERMINATE signal. The plan specifies threading only at the `finalize_verdict` call site (line 209) and the `base` dict (lines 141-147), but `base` is used in the bug-tier return (line 171) only via `{**base, ...}` — and since `base` at that point (line 141-147) does not yet include `hierarchy_incomplete`, the bug-tier output is missing both fields.
scenarios: A bug-type ticket is reviewed; its child enumeration fails (hierarchy_incomplete=True); the LLM pass fails mid-run; _recover_plan_review_coach_failure or _recover_plan_review_verify_failure runs; it reads precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False) → False; the recovered verdict is PASS instead of INDETERMINATE.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `hierarchy_incomplete: False` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: []` to the initial `base` dict at lines 141-147. Then, after `pctx` is assembled (line 140), overwrite: `base['hierarchy_incomplete'] = pctx.hierarchy_incomplete` and `base['hierarchy_incomplete_detail'] = pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail`. This ensures every return path (exempt, bug-tier, P8-too-big, normal) includes the fields in its output dict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the insertion order of the `hierarchy_incomplete` branch relative to `llm_unavailable`/`verify_failed` in both `finalize_verdict` and `_recompute_verdict_after_drop`, and document the tiebreak rule when multiple flags are simultaneously truthy (e.g., 'hierarchy_incomplete is checked last, so verify_failed takes precedence').
evidence: 'add elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE" alongside the existing llm_unavailable/verify_failed branches (same elif chain, checked before falling through to PASS)' — the relative ordering within that chain is not stated.
Multi-flag scenarios (hierarchy incomplete AND verify failed, or incomplete AND llm unavailable) have no specified winner.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify where in the existing `elif` chain the new `hierarchy_incomplete` branch must be inserted in `finalize_verdict` and `drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop` relative to the existing `llm_unavailable` and `verify_failed` branches. If multiple flags are simultaneously truthy (e.g., hierarchy incomplete AND verify failed), the ordering determines which verdict wins, but no tiebreak rule is stated.
findings.location: Remediation items 3 and 4; AC line: 'finalize_verdict ... and drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop ... each gain an elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE" branch'
scenarios: A recovery path sets both verify_failed=True and hierarchy_incomplete=True; which branch fires first determines the emitted verdict, affecting downstream audit trails.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation items 3 and 4, add: 'Insert the `elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete")` branch AFTER the existing `llm_unavailable` and `verify_failed` branches so that explicit failure signals take precedence over incomplete-context signals; when both apply, the more specific failure wins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add two retry-behavior tests to the test-coverage AC: (1) first list_tickets raises then succeeds on retry -> hierarchy_incomplete remains False and children are populated; (2) both attempts raise -> hierarchy_incomplete=True, hierarchy_incomplete_detail==['enumeration'] and retry count is exactly 2 (assert mock called twice, not once or infinitely).
evidence: Remediation item 1: '2 attempts total (1 retry), catching the same broad Exception it catches today, with a fixed 50ms delay between attempts'

AC bullets specify behavior of retry (set hierarchy_incomplete on exhaustion) but no test asserts the retry fires exactly once before exhaustion, nor that a transient failure followed by success leaves hierarchy_incomplete=False
Absence: test-coverage AC (last bullet) lists only total-failure and partial-child-failure scenarios — no retry-succeeds or retry-count-bounded scenario
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bounded retry (2 attempts, 50ms delay) as new conditional logic in _assemble_context_uncached, but no test is specified for the retry-succeeds path (first list_tickets call raises, second succeeds) or for confirming the retry is bounded (exactly 2 attempts, not infinite, before the flag is set). These are distinct branches in the new retry logic with no corresponding test work called out.
findings.location: AC bullets 1–3 and Remediation item 1 (retry specification); Acceptance Criteria test-coverage bullet (item a/b/c) — absence of retry behavioral tests
scenarios: Retry is accidentally implemented as a while-True loop with no bound; tests pass because total-failure test still sets hierarchy_incomplete=True eventually, but under load the function hangs

First call raises, second succeeds — hierarchy_incomplete should be False (context loaded successfully on retry); if the implementation instead sets the flag on any exception (before retrying), this path is wrong and no test catches it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state: either (a) document that `lint_refs.py`/validation reads the recorded dict permissively (not against the schema) so extra fields are harmless after rollback, OR (b) specify that rollback must be compensated-forward (keep new fields in schema even if feature is disabled), OR (c) add an explicit DATA LOSS RISK note and a migration step to strip the new fields from persisted records before schema rollback.
evidence: Plan states: 'Leave `additionalProperties: false` as-is (both new keys are now declared)' and 'existing recorded verdicts / older step outputs without the field stay schema-valid' — forward compat only, rollback direction unaddressed.
plan_review_precheck_output.schema.json line 17: `"additionalProperties": false` — any field not in `properties` will fail validation.

gate_dispatch.py ~line 316: `precheck = succeeded.get(STEP_PRECHECK)` reads persisted step outputs back from run records; if rollback removes the new fields from `properties`, records written by the new code become invalid.
ABSENCE: No rollback-post-state assertion in the plan — no statement like 'on rollback, records containing the new fields are handled via [mechanism]' or DATA LOSS RISK acknowledgement.
findings.finding: The plan asserts forward compatibility (old records without new fields remain valid) but does not assert the post-rollback schema state. After rollback, persisted run records that were written by the new code — and therefore contain `hierarchy_incomplete` / `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` — will violate the rolled-back schema's `additionalProperties: false` constraint. Recovery paths in `gate_dispatch.py` that read back `precheck = succeeded.get(STEP_PRECHECK)` from such records may encounter validation errors or unexpected behaviour.
scenarios: Deploy new code → a run executes `plan_review_precheck` and records outputs containing `hierarchy_incomplete: true` → rollback removes new fields from schema → a recovery path reads that recorded precheck output and schema-validates it → `additionalProperties: false` rejects the record → recovery fails or falls back to a wrong verdict.
A run spans a deploy boundary (precheck recorded under new code, recovery invoked under rolled-back old code) — the recorded precheck dict has undeclared fields that the old schema rejects.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or justify why one is unnecessary) for the PASS→INDETERMINATE behavioral change in `finalize_verdict` and `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` — e.g. a named feature flag, a one-line config default that restores the old path, or a documented revert commit procedure with safe-revert conditions.
evidence: Remediation item 3: 'add elif coverage.get("hierarchy_incomplete"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"' — changes finalize_verdict output for the failure path from PASS to INDETERMINATE
Remediation item 4: '_recompute_verdict_after_drop … add the identical elif … verdict["verdict"] = "INDETERMINATE"' — same behavioral change in drift/xcheck paths
No section of the plan describes how to revert if INDETERMINATE floods production verdicts unexpectedly (e.g. a transient Jira outage hitting every plan-review run)
AC #9 and Remediation item 6 justify the change but provide no back-out path
findings.finding: The plan changes `finalize_verdict` and `drift_floor._recompute_verdict_after_drop` so that previously-silent hierarchy-load failures now produce `INDETERMINATE` instead of `PASS`. This is a behavioral breaking change for any downstream consumer, workflow gate, or test that previously received a clean PASS when enumeration silently failed. The plan justifies the change (Remediation item 6, AC #9) but specifies no rollback/back-out plan and no expand-contract sequence — T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement.
scenarios: A transient ticket-store outage causes list_tickets to fail on every call; all in-flight plan reviews return INDETERMINATE and block merges; there is no described feature-flag or config knob to revert to the old silent-pass behaviour while the outage is investigated.
A test suite that mocked enumeration failures and asserted PASS now fails; no incremental migration path is described.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single boolean config/env var `REBAR_HIERARCHY_INCOMPLETE_BLOCKS` (default `True`). When `False`, `finalize_verdict` and `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` skip the `hierarchy_incomplete` branch and behave as today. Document that setting it `False` restores pre-change behaviour as an incident-response lever. This is a two-line conditional change and avoids a full revert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the retry attempt count and inter-attempt delay into named module-level constants (e.g., `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_ATTEMPTS = 2`, `_HIERARCHY_RETRY_DELAY_S = 0.05`) so they can be found and adjusted in one place without hunting through prose.
evidence: 'wrap the list_tickets call (line 174) in a bounded retry: 2 attempts total (1 retry), catching the same broad Exception it catches today, with a fixed 50ms delay between attempts'
No mention of a config constant, environment variable, or named module-level default for either value.
findings.finding: The 50ms inter-attempt delay and retry count (2 attempts total) are hardcoded constants with no configuration point. If ticket-store read latency changes (e.g., store moves to a slower backend, or CI environments have higher jitter), these values would need a code change rather than a config/env update.
scenarios: CI environment with high I/O jitter causes false INDETERMINATE verdicts because 50ms is too short.
Ticket store moves to a networked backend and 50ms is insufficient, but changing it requires a code deploy rather than a config update.
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of `orchestrator.py` add `_HIERARCHY_FETCH_ATTEMPTS: int = 2` and `_HIERARCHY_RETRY_DELAY_S: float = 0.05`; reference them in the retry loop. This keeps the values hardcoded-but-named, satisfying changeability without introducing env-var complexity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md or inline architecture note (e.g., a module-level docstring in orchestrator.py or a brief ADR) capturing: (1) the coverage-dict threading contract for finalize_verdict, (2) why PlanContext is not the threading vehicle for coach/recovery sites, and (3) the no-carve-out decision with a pointer to parent epic AC #4 — so future call-site authors know the invariant without reading the ticket.
evidence: Five finalize_verdict call sites across workflow_ops.py and gate_dispatch.py, each requiring different sourcing logic (real pctx vs. precheck dict vs. ctx param)
Remediation item 6 documents the carve-out rationale inline in the ticket, but the ticket body is not a durable artifact consulted during future development
No AC line requires an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc section
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-cutting architectural pattern — a `hierarchy_incomplete` flag threaded through 5 `finalize_verdict` call sites across 4 files via the `coverage` dict — but does not call for an ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update to capture (a) why the coverage dict (not the PlanContext object) is the threading mechanism, (b) why no relevance-exception carve-out is implemented, and (c) the exact call-site topology future maintainers must update when adding a new finalize_verdict site.
findings.location: Remediation items 2, 3, 6 / AC — no documentation update AC present
scenarios: A developer adds a sixth finalize_verdict call site (e.g., a new recovery path) and doesn't know to thread hierarchy_incomplete through coverage, silently allowing INDETERMINATE to revert to PASS on that path.
A reviewer asks why the coverage dict is used instead of enriching PlanContext and passing it through — the rationale exists only in the ticket, not in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `logger.warning(...)` call (or equivalent structured log) in each `except` branch of `_assemble_context_uncached` that sets `hierarchy_incomplete=True`, including the failed child id or `'enumeration'` and the exception repr, so operators can correlate INDETERMINATE verdicts to specific read failures without inspecting raw coverage dicts.
evidence: Remediation item 1 specifies setting hierarchy_incomplete=True and hierarchy_incomplete_detail=[...] but contains no 'log a warning' or 'emit a metric' instruction
No AC line requires a log line, metric, or alert for the catch-and-flag paths
finalize_verdict already emits verdict=INDETERMINATE for llm_unavailable/verify_failed but the plan adds no similar log distinguishing the hierarchy-failure case
findings.finding: The plan adds new failure points (`list_tickets` and `show_ticket` read failures) that set `hierarchy_incomplete=True` and ultimately produce INDETERMINATE verdicts, but specifies no logging, metric, or trace at the point of failure. Operators receiving an INDETERMINATE verdict cannot distinguish a ticket-store read failure from any other INDETERMINATE cause without inspecting coverage dicts in stored step outputs.
scenarios: Ticket store becomes intermittently unavailable; all plan reviews return INDETERMINATE; on-call engineer sees INDETERMINATE verdicts but has no metric/log to correlate them to ticket-store read failures without reading raw step output dicts.

A bug in list_tickets silently degrades all container-ticket plan reviews to INDETERMINATE; without a log line at the catch site, the failure is invisible until someone manually inspects stored coverage.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `except` block for `list_tickets` failure: `logger.warning('hierarchy_context_load_failed', extra={'ticket_id': canonical, 'detail': 'enumeration', 'exc': repr(exc)})`. In the per-child `except`: `logger.warning('hierarchy_context_load_failed', extra={'ticket_id': canonical, 'child_id': child_id, 'exc': repr(exc)})`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` `hierarchy_incomplete` branch (Remediation item 4) is needed: document the specific scenario that exercises it (LLM-tier BLOCK finding drift-dropped with hierarchy_incomplete in coverage), or narrow the scope of item 4 to avoid adding dead code and its associated test burden.
evidence: drift_floor.py:199: `if verdict.get('verdict') != 'BLOCK' or verdict.get('blocking'): return` — the guard bails for any non-BLOCK verdict, so an already-INDETERMINATE verdict is left unchanged (correct), and a BLOCK verdict with non-empty blocking also bails (the only way blocking could be empty is if LLM blocking findings were drift-dropped)
Plan Evidence: 'A DET block is never novel (the novelty sub-call never scores it), so it survives as blocking and keeps the verdict at BLOCK' — confirms DET blocks can't be drift-dropped

finalize_verdict verdict logic: `if blocking: verdict = 'BLOCK'` takes precedence over `hierarchy_incomplete`, so a co-occurring DET block and hierarchy_incomplete yields BLOCK; after drift-floor, the DET block remains in blocking, guard bails, new branch never reached
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation item 4 adds an `elif cov.get('hierarchy_incomplete'): verdict['verdict'] = 'INDETERMINATE'` branch to `_recompute_verdict_after_drop`, but this branch is unreachable in practice. `_recompute_verdict_after_drop` only runs its body when `verdict.get('verdict') == 'BLOCK'` AND `not verdict.get('blocking')` — i.e., only when a BLOCK verdict has just had its last blocking finding dropped. The plan's own Evidence confirms DET blocks are never novel and therefore never drift-dropped, so any `hierarchy_incomplete` co-occurring with a BLOCK verdict would mean the DET block survives and `verdict.get('blocking')` remains truthy, causing the guard to bail before the new branch. The drift_floor change is inert code that adds test-coverage burden (the AC requires testing it through all three call sites) for zero real-world benefit.
findings.location: Remediation item 4 / drift_floor.py:190-211 / AC: 'the drift_floor branch is exercised through all three call sites'
scenarios: LLM blocking finding (non-DET) + hierarchy_incomplete: finalize_verdict yields BLOCK (blocking wins); drift-floor drops the LLM finding leaving blocking=[]; _recompute_verdict_after_drop runs; the new hierarchy_incomplete branch fires. This is the only path that reaches the new branch — but it requires an LLM blocking finding to be drift-dropped, which itself requires the LLM tier to have run (contradicting the typical hierarchy_incomplete+DET-only scenario). This is a theoretically possible but extremely unlikely edge case.

findings.finding: `PlanContext` is a `@dataclass` (det_floor.py:103), so `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` is a mutable default that will raise `ValueError: mutable default <class 'list'> for field hierarchy_incomplete_detail is not allowed: use default_factory` at class-definition time. The plan's AC #1 and Remediation item 1 both specify the bare `= []` form, which is invalid. The existing parallel list field `children` already uses `field(default_factory=list)` as the correct pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: In `det_floor.py`, declare: `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)` (and update AC #1 wording accordingly).

findings.finding: The plan specifies `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` as a new field on `PlanContext` but does not specify it must use `dataclasses.field(default_factory=list)` (or equivalent). If `PlanContext` is a dataclass and the field is declared with a bare `[]` literal, Python raises `ValueError` at class-definition time. If `PlanContext` is a plain class and the field is set as a class-level attribute `hierarchy_incomplete_detail = []`, all instances share the same list object — `_assemble_context_uncached`'s `append` calls in the per-child failure path would mutate that shared default across invocations, silently corrupting `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` for every subsequent `PlanContext` created in the same process (e.g., under the cached `assemble_context` wrapper or in batch-review scenarios).
findings.location: Remediation item 1 / AC line: "`PlanContext` gains `hierarchy_incomplete: bool = False` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []`"
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC and Remediation item 1, change the field declaration to: `hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)` (for a dataclass) or add `self.hierarchy_incomplete_detail: list[str] = []` in `PlanContext.__init__` (for a plain class).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add `hierarchy_incomplete` and `hierarchy_incomplete_detail` to the inline `coverage` dict at the `p8_too_big` → `finalize_verdict` call (workflow_ops.py ~line 213), reading from `pctx`, in addition to the `base` dict update already specified in Remediation item 2.
evidence: workflow_ops.py:209: `coverage={'det': det_cov, 'llm_ran': False}` — a standalone dict literal, not derived from `base`

Remediation item 2 says 'plan_review_precheck ... reads pctx.hierarchy_incomplete/pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail directly ... and threads both into its base/coverage dicts' — the p8_too_big inline coverage dict is a third, separate coverage construction at the same call site that is not mentioned

gate_dispatch.py recovery paths at lines 324-329 and 381-389 source hierarchy_incomplete from `precheck.get(...)` where precheck is the recorded step output — if the p8_too_big path omits the flag, those recovery sites receive False silently
findings.finding: The `p8_too_big` short-circuit arm in `plan_review_precheck` (workflow_ops.py ~line 209) calls `finalize_verdict` with an inline, standalone `coverage` literal `{'det': det_cov, 'llm_ran': False}` that is NOT derived from `base` and therefore would NOT carry `hierarchy_incomplete`/`hierarchy_incomplete_detail` under the plan's Remediation item 2 as written. If hierarchy enumeration fails on a P8-too-big plan, `finalize_verdict` would produce BLOCK (DET block wins) rather than INDETERMINATE, which is arguable — but the `hierarchy_incomplete` flag would be silently absent from `coverage`, breaking the downstream recovery paths that rely on `precheck.get('hierarchy_incomplete')` at the three gate_dispatch sites.
findings.location: Remediation item 2 / workflow_ops.py:202-217 (p8_too_big branch, finalize_verdict call at line 209)
scenarios: A container ticket with P8-too-big AND a failed child enumeration: the p8_too_big verdict is emitted without `hierarchy_incomplete` in coverage; the returned `base` dict (which the plan does update) has the field, but the inline `finalize_verdict` coverage does not, so the embedded `verdict.coverage` object is missing the flag
findings.suggested_fix: Change the inline dict from `coverage={'det': det_cov, 'llm_ran': False}` to `coverage={'det': det_cov, 'llm_ran': False, 'hierarchy_incomplete': pctx.hierarchy_incomplete, 'hierarchy_incomplete_detail': pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail}` at workflow_ops.py ~line 213.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC (c) to require at least one test per threading mechanism: one test exercising a dict-sourced call site (e.g., plan_review_coach reading hierarchy_incomplete via ctx.inputs.get) producing INDETERMINATE, in addition to the existing direct-pctx-path test — ensuring dict-threading bugs don't silently lose the flag.
evidence: 'at at least one of the five finalize_verdict sites' — plan AC text, last bullet

Remediation item 2 explicitly identifies two distinct threading mechanisms: direct pctx attribute read (precheck, _degraded path) vs dict-sourced via ctx.inputs.get / precheck.get (plan_review_coach, _recover_plan_review_coach_failure, _recover_plan_review_verify_failure)

A dict-key-missing bug (e.g., hierarchy_incomplete omitted from the precheck step's returned base dict) would cause ctx.inputs.get('hierarchy_incomplete', False) to return False, silently passing — undetected if only the direct-ctx path is tested
findings.finding: The test-coverage AC requires verdict=INDETERMINATE 'at least one of the five finalize_verdict sites', leaving the dict-threading call sites (plan_review_coach sourcing from ctx.inputs, _recover_plan_review_coach_failure and _recover_plan_review_verify_failure sourcing from precheck dict) with no explicit test. These three paths use a different threading mechanism than the direct-pctx path (_degraded_plan_review_verdict), and a bug — e.g., hierarchy_incomplete not stored in the precheck base dict, or misread via ctx.inputs.get — would silently drop the flag and let verdict revert to PASS, exactly the failure mode the plan is designed to prevent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, last bullet (item c): 'both cases -> verdict is INDETERMINATE at at least one of the five finalize_verdict sites'
scenarios: hierarchy_incomplete is set correctly in _assemble_context_uncached but never stored in the precheck output base dict; plan_review_coach and both recovery paths silently read False; verdict is PASS instead of INDETERMINATE
Key is stored correctly but consumed via a wrong key name in one recovery path; dict-sourced path reads empty while direct-ctx path works
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'at least one of the five finalize_verdict sites' with 'including at least one dict-sourced call site (plan_review_coach or a _recover_* path) and at least one direct-ctx call site (plan_review_precheck or _degraded_plan_review_verdict)' in the last AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_reads.show_ticket`/`list_tickets` exhibit observed transient failures on local ticket-store reads that justify a retry; if no such evidence exists, remove the retry loop and set `hierarchy_incomplete=True` immediately on first failure (the flag and fallback behavior are correct regardless — the retry adds only latency and complexity).
evidence: Plan: 'a bespoke tiny retry is justified, specified concretely below' — but no observed flakiness is cited for local ticket-store reads
`_reads.py:53-62`: `show_ticket` maps ALL `ReadError` to `RebarError` with no transient/permanent split — permanent errors (missing ticket) would be retried
Plan's own evidence: 'no reusable predicate/backoff precedent applies to this call' — the reason no predicate exists is that the store doesn't surface transient vs permanent errors

`_call_with_retry` (`dispatch_one.py:113-139`) uses typed predicates (JiraAPIError 429/5xx, HTTPError 429/5xx, TimeoutError) specifically to avoid retrying permanent 4xx — this discipline is absent from the proposed retry

The primary effect of the retry is the `hierarchy_incomplete` flag set on exhaustion — which is equally achievable on the FIRST failure with no retry at all, eliminating the latency and misfired-retry concerns
findings.finding: The proposed bespoke 2-attempt retry (50ms fixed delay, broad `except Exception`) in `_assemble_context_uncached` for `list_tickets` and `show_ticket` retries on permanent failures as well as transient ones — yet the plan cites no evidence that local ticket-store reads are transiently unreliable. `_reads.show_ticket` raises `RebarError` on any failure (no transient/permanent split per `_reads.py:53-62`), meaning a missing-ticket `ReadError` would be retried once unnecessarily before being flagged `hierarchy_incomplete`. The plan's own evidence states 'No reusable predicate/backoff precedent applies' and `_call_with_retry` uses typed predicates (JiraAPIError/HTTPError/TimeoutError) specifically to avoid retrying permanent errors. Adding an untyped retry on a local file-based store — without citing observed flakiness — risks masking real errors and adds latency (50ms × 2 sites × N children per review) with no demonstrated benefit.
scenarios: A ticket whose child id is malformed or deleted causes `show_ticket` to raise `RebarError` permanently; the retry adds 50ms of latency before the already-correct fallback (summary-row) is applied.

A review of a container ticket with 10 children where all children exist but one is malformed: 10 unnecessary 50ms sleeps (500ms added latency) on a code-path the plan notes runs on 'a worker thread' during fan-out.

A total `list_tickets` permanent failure (e.g. corrupted tracker state) is retried once, adding 50ms before the same `hierarchy_incomplete=True` outcome that would be reached immediately without the retry.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the retry loop. On first `Exception` from `list_tickets`, set `pctx.hierarchy_incomplete = True`, `pctx.hierarchy_incomplete_detail = ["enumeration"]`, and continue with `listed = []` (existing behavior). On per-child `show_ticket` failure, append the child id to `hierarchy_incomplete_detail`, set `hierarchy_incomplete = True`, and continue with the summary-row fallback (existing behavior). The AC's correctness goal — INDETERMINATE on hierarchy load failure — is fully achieved without any sleep.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
